_work.title: Folk Magic, Apotropaic & Protective Practice
_work.creator: various
_work.place: Europe and the wider Mediterranean / Atlantic world
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY-SA / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The everyday occult of cunning-folk and householders — the apotropaic marks scratched on barns and church timbers (the daisy-wheel hexafoil, taper burn-marks), Pennsylvania-Dutch hex signs, the evil-eye amulets of the Mediterranean (nazar, cornicello, mano cornuta, fascinum, the cimaruta), horseshoes and witch-balls hung against harm, poppets and hag-stones, the masks of Krampus and the Alpine Perchten, the SATOR square and abracadabra charms, Ethiopian magic scrolls, and the printed pow-wow charm-books of the Pennsylvania Germans.

entries.title: Chalk hexafoil (daisy-wheel) apotropaic mark
entries.creator: Anonymous (folk tradition); photograph by Violetcabra
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, photo: Violetcabra). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A hexafoil or 'daisy wheel', the six-petalled rosette compass-scribed onto buildings as a protective 'witch mark'.

## Iconography
A circle within which six arcs, struck from points on the circumference at the radius, interlace to form a **six-petalled flower**. The figure is drawn in a single continuous compass operation, leaving no gap for a spirit to enter.

## Significance
The **hexafoil** is the commonest of the British [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] marks catalogued by surveys of ritual-protection graffiti (notably Matthew Champion's work on medieval church graffiti). Cut into door-frames, beams, and fireplaces, the unbroken interlace was held to trap or repel malevolent spirits and the evil eye. Its solar-rosette geometry links it to far older protective sun-wheels.

## For artists & game designers
A single-stroke geometric ward that a character can scratch anywhere with a compass or knife — instantly readable as 'protective sigil'.

citations.text: Matthew Champion, *Medieval Graffiti: The Lost Voices of England's Churches* (2015).
Timothy Easton, 'Apotropaic Symbols and Other Measures for Protecting Buildings against Misfortune,' in *Physical Evidence for Ritual Acts* (2016).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Taper burn marks, Haddon Hall
entries.creator: Anonymous (folk tradition); photograph by Delusion23
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Delusion23). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Deliberate taper burn-marks on timber at Haddon Hall, a form of apotropaic charring made to guard a building.

## Iconography
Clusters of **teardrop-shaped scorch marks** burned into oak beams and door-jambs with the flame of a candle or taper. The marks are shallow, repeated, and concentrated around openings — doorways, windows, and especially fireplaces.

## Significance
Long mistaken for accidental scorching, these **taper burn-marks** are now recognised by historians of vernacular building as a deliberate [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] practice: by symbolically 'burning' the timber, householders were thought to inoculate it against destructive fire and against the malevolent spirits that travelled by chimney and threshold. Timothy Easton's fieldwork established their intentional, patterned distribution.

## For artists & game designers
Scorch-marks deliberately burned around a hearth as a fire-ward — atmospheric environmental lore that hints at unseen folk belief.

citations.text: Timothy Easton, 'Candle Powers,' *Cornerstone* 33 (2012).
John Dean & Nick Hill, 'Burn Marks on Buildings,' *Vernacular Architecture* (2014).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Pennsylvania barn with hex signs, Oley (HABS)
entries.creator: Pennsylvania German tradition; HABS photograph by Charles Dornbusch
entries.rights: Public domain (HABS, Charles Dornbusch). Library of Congress, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Pennsylvania-German bank barn at Oley painted with hex signs, the geometric 'barn stars' of Pennsylvania-Dutch folk decoration.

## Iconography
Large circular **rosette stars** painted on the barn's gable and façade — radiating points within concentric rings, in bright colour. The motifs descend from the compass-rosette and Fraktur ornament of the Rhineland settlers.

## Significance
The **hex sign** (*Hexefoos*) is the best-known American folk-magical mark, though its protective meaning is much debated: nineteenth-century barn stars were partly decorative and partly, in popular belief, [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] — guarding the barn, the livestock, and the harvest. The term 'hex' links them in the popular imagination to the Pennsylvania-German pow-wow (*Brauche*) tradition. Scholarship by Don Yoder and Thomas Graves disentangles ornament from charm.

## For artists & game designers
Bold painted star-rosettes on a rural building: an immediately legible regional 'folk ward' aesthetic.

citations.text: Don Yoder & Thomas E. Graves, *Hex Signs: Pennsylvania Dutch Barn Symbols and Their Meaning* (2000).
Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS), Library of Congress.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Nazar (evil-eye) glass amulet, Turkey
entries.creator: Anonymous (Turkish glassmaker); photograph by Zorka Sojka
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Zorka Sojka). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A nazar boncuğu, the blue glass eye-bead hung throughout the eastern Mediterranean to ward off the evil eye.

## Iconography
A flattened disc of **blue glass** marked with **concentric rings** — white, light blue, and a dark central pupil — forming a stylised staring **eye**. The cobalt blue is itself held to be protective.

## Significance
The **nazar** answers the ancient and widespread fear of the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] (*nazar*, *baskania*, *malocchio*): a covetous or envious glance believed to bring harm. By presenting an eye that 'looks back', the amulet reflects or absorbs the malevolent gaze. The belief is documented from antiquity (Plutarch, the *Mishnah*) to the present and crosses the Islamic, Jewish, and Christian Mediterranean alike.

## For artists & game designers
The glowing blue eye-bead is one of the world's most recognisable protective charms — a compact 'anti-curse' object with instant cultural legibility.

citations.text: Alan Dundes (ed.), *The Evil Eye: A Casebook* (1981).
Frederick Thomas Elworthy, *The Evil Eye* (1895).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Cornicello (Italian horn charm)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Italian tradition); photograph by Antonio Cavallo
entries.rights: Public domain (photo: Antonio Cavallo). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A cornicello, the twisted red horn-shaped amulet of southern Italian folk magic, worn against the malocchio.

## Iconography
A slender, **twisted horn** tapering to a point, traditionally of red **coral**, gold, or silver, pierced for suspension. The form recalls an animal horn or a chilli pepper.

## Significance
The **cornicello** ('little horn') is the classic Neapolitan charm against the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] (*malocchio*) and against *iettatura*, ill-luck cast by an envious glance. Its protective force draws on the ancient apotropaic power of the horn and of red coral, both attested in Roman amulets. Frederick Elworthy and later folklorists traced its descent from antique horn-and-phallus charms.

## For artists & game designers
A small red twisted horn on a chain — a wearable luck-charm with deep Mediterranean roots and a striking silhouette.

citations.text: Frederick Thomas Elworthy, *The Evil Eye* (1895).
Alan Dundes (ed.), *The Evil Eye: A Casebook* (1981).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Mano cornuta amulet (horned-hand gesture)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Italian tradition); photograph by Ra Boe
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: Ra Boe). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An amulet in the form of the mano cornuta, the 'horned hand' whose extended index and little finger make the protective corna sign.

## Iconography
A cast **hand** with the index and little finger extended and the middle two folded under the thumb — the *corna* or 'horns'. Rendered in metal or coral, it is worn as a pendant; as a living gesture it is made with the hand pointed downward or outward.

## Significance
The **mano cornuta** is one of the principal Italian gestures and amulets against the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]]. The horned silhouette echoes the apotropaic horn (as in the [[motif:cornicello|cornicello]]); pointed at a suspected *jettatore*, it deflects the malevolent gaze. It belongs with the *mano fico* (fig-hand) in the repertory of protective hand-signs catalogued since Andrea de Jorio's study of Neapolitan gesture.

## For artists & game designers
A frozen protective gesture cast in metal — a charm that encodes a ritual movement, good for a 'gesture of warding' motif.

citations.text: Andrea de Jorio, *La mimica degli antichi investigata nel gestire napoletano* (1832).
Frederick Thomas Elworthy, *The Evil Eye* (1895).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Roman phallic amulet (fascinum)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman); recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (Portable Antiquities Scheme, Colchester & Ipswich Museum). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Roman copper-alloy phallic pendant or fascinum, recorded by the Portable Antiquities Scheme, worn as protection against the evil eye.

## Iconography
A small cast **copper-alloy** amulet modelled as a **phallus**, pierced or looped for suspension. Many such pieces combine the phallus with a fist or the *mano fica* on the reverse, doubling the apotropaic charge.

## Significance
The **fascinum** was the Roman embodiment of the divine apotropaic power that bore the same name; phallic charms were hung on children, soldiers, and doorways to deflect the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] (*invidia*) and the harm of envy. Pliny records the practice, and the type underlies the medieval and modern Italian [[motif:cornicello|horn]] and [[motif:mano cornuta|hand]] charms. Such finds are common across the Roman provinces.

## For artists & game designers
An ancient pocket charm that wards by drawing the eye to a shocking image — the historical ancestor of later luck-horns.

citations.text: Pliny the Elder, *Natural History*, on the fascinum.
Catherine Johns, *Sex or Symbol: Erotic Images of Greece and Rome* (1982).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Cimaruta (sprig-of-rue charm), from Elworthy
entries.creator: Frederick Thomas Elworthy (illustration)
entries.rights: Public domain (Frederick Thomas Elworthy, 1895). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The cimaruta, the silver 'sprig of rue' charm of Italian folk magic, hung over cradles against the evil eye, as illustrated in Elworthy's *The Evil Eye*.

## Iconography
A branching silver charm shaped like a sprig of **rue**, from whose three stems hang a cluster of protective symbols: a **crescent moon**, a **key**, a **serpent**, a flower, a hand, a fish, and others. Each pendant adds its own apotropaic virtue.

## Significance
The **cimaruta** (*cima di ruta*, 'top of rue') compounds many charms into one: rue itself was a protective herb, and the moon, key, and serpent invoke Diana, Hecate, and older guardian powers. Hung over an infant's bed, it guarded against the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] and witches. Elworthy's 1895 study made it the type-specimen of the Italian compound amulet, later central to discussions of survivals of pagan goddess-cult.

## For artists & game designers
A single charm that bundles a dozen protective symbols on one stem — a richly detailed 'composite amulet' for close study.

citations.text: Frederick Thomas Elworthy, *The Evil Eye* (1895).
Charles Godfrey Leland, *Etruscan Roman Remains in Popular Tradition* (1892).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: The 'Evil Eye' / Lucky Hunchback mosaic, Antioch
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman); photograph by Carole Raddato
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 2.0 (photo: Carole Raddato). Hatay Archaeology Museum, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Roman floor mosaic from Antioch showing the evil eye under attack, a threshold-charm against the malevolent gaze.

## Iconography
A large **eye** is assailed from every side — pierced by a **trident** and sword, set upon by a **scorpion**, serpent, dog, and birds — while a horned, ithyphallic dwarf (the 'Lucky Hunchback') strides away. The composition shows the destruction of the eye's power.

## Significance
Laid at a doorway, this mosaic is monumental [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] art: the assembled creatures and weapons turn the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] against itself, while the hunchback (*gibbus*) and phallus add their own luck-bringing, eye-deflecting force. It is among the most cited ancient depictions of the belief, a direct ancestor of the later [[motif:nazar|nazar]] and horn charms.

## For artists & game designers
A whole bestiary marshalled to stab a single eye — a vivid template for an 'anti-curse ward' rendered as a scene rather than a symbol.

citations.text: Doro Levi, *Antioch Mosaic Pavements* (1947).
Katherine M. D. Dunbabin, *Mosaics of the Greek and Roman World* (1999).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Lucky horseshoe over a door
entries.creator: Anonymous (folk tradition); photograph by Man vyi
entries.rights: Public domain (photo: Man vyi). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A horseshoe nailed over a doorway, the most familiar of European luck-and-protection charms.

## Iconography
An iron **horseshoe** fixed above a **door**, conventionally with the points turned **upward** (so the luck 'does not run out') — though points-down, to pour protection over those entering, is equally traditional.

## Significance
The **horseshoe** unites several strands of folk belief: the protective virtue of **iron** against witches and fairies, the sanctity attached to the smith and to Saint Dunstan (who legendarily shod the Devil), and the lucky crescent shape. Nailed at the [[motif:apotropaic|threshold]], it guards the house against bewitchment. It is among the longest-surviving and most widespread of everyday charms.

## For artists & game designers
The humblest possible ward: a piece of iron over a door whose orientation carries meaning — easy environmental storytelling.

citations.text: Edward A. Armstrong, 'The Symbolism of the Horseshoe,' *Folklore* (1944).
Owen Davies, *Popular Magic: Cunning-folk in English History* (2003).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Glass witch-ball (New England Glass Company), Met
entries.creator: New England Glass Company
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0). The Metropolitan Museum of Art, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A blown-glass witch-ball with its vase, a reflective sphere hung in windows to repel witchcraft and the evil eye.

## Iconography
A hollow, often **iridescent or coloured glass sphere**, here paired with a matching vase. Its mirror-bright, curved surface throws back a distorted reflection of whatever approaches.

## Significance
The **witch-ball** worked by reflection and fascination: hung in a window, its shining surface was believed to dazzle and turn back the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]] or to trap a witch's malevolent gaze, much as the threads inside a glass 'spirit ball' were said to ensnare spirits. The form spread from English folk practice into American decorative glass, blurring charm and ornament — kin to the silvered 'gazing ball'.

## For artists & game designers
A pretty reflective orb that doubles as a ward by bouncing back a curse — decorative object with a hidden protective function.

citations.text: Ralph Merrifield, *The Archaeology of Ritual and Magic* (1987).
Metropolitan Museum of Art, witch-ball and vase.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Doll pierced with pins, Museum of Witchcraft
entries.creator: Anonymous (folk-magical artefact); photograph by Midnightblueowl
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, photo: Midnightblueowl). Museum of Witchcraft, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A cloth poppet stuck with pins, the classic image-magic doll, in the Museum of Witchcraft and Magic at Boscastle.

## Iconography
A crude **fabric figure** in human shape, into which **pins** have been driven. The doll stands in for a specific person; what is done to it is meant to befall them.

## Significance
The **poppet** (also 'mommet' or 'corp creadh') is the European instrument of sympathetic [[motif:image magic|image magic]], used both to curse — piercing to cause pain — and, inverted, to heal or protect. Such dolls appear in early-modern witch-trial evidence and survived in cunning-folk practice into the twentieth century. The Boscastle museum holds one of the richest collections of these objects, studied by Owen Davies and others.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal curse-object: a pin-stuck doll that visibly links harm to a victim through likeness — instantly readable image magic.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Popular Magic: Cunning-folk in English History* (2003).
Museum of Witchcraft and Magic, Boscastle.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: The hag-stone / adder-stone (holed stone charm)
entries.creator: Anonymous (folk tradition); diagram via Wikimedia
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An illustration concerning the hag-stone or adder-stone, the naturally holed pebble used across Britain and northern Europe as a protective charm.

## Iconography
The figure concerns the **holed stone** — a pebble or flint naturally pierced by water-wear. Such stones were hung on bedposts, stable doors, and keys, or peered through.

## Significance
The **hag-stone** (also 'adder-stone', *Hühnergott*) was held to guard against the night-hag or [[motif:nightmare|Mara]] who pressed sleepers, against witches who rode horses by night, and against the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]]; looking through the hole was said to grant sight of fairies or the truth behind glamour. The naturally formed hole made it a charm 'made by nature', and it remained one of the commonest free-found amulets in European folk magic.

## For artists & game designers
A found pebble with a hole that reveals the unseen when looked through — a perfect low-magic 'true-sight' item.

citations.text: Christina Hole, *A Dictionary of British Folk Customs* (1976).
Ralph Merrifield, *The Archaeology of Ritual and Magic* (1987).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Krampus masks of Austria
entries.creator: Anonymous (Alpine mask-carvers); photograph by Naturpuur
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Naturpuur). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Carved wooden Krampus masks from Austria, the horned figures of the Alpine midwinter processions.

## Iconography
Fiercely carved **wooden masks** with great curling **horns**, bared fangs, lolling tongues, and wild fur, worn with bells and birch switches. Each mask is hand-carved and individual.

## Significance
**Krampus** is the horned companion of St Nicholas in the Alpine *Krampuslauf*, punishing the wicked while Nicholas rewards the good. He belongs to the same midwinter complex as the [[motif:perchten|Perchten]]: masked, noise-making processions that drive out winter and evil spirits. Though Christianised, the custom preserves much older apotropaic mumming, in which the terrifying mask both embodies and repels malevolent forces. Studied by John Honigmann and folklorists of Alpine custom.

## For artists & game designers
A gallery of hand-carved horned masks — a deep reference for terrifying-yet-protective folk monster design.

citations.text: John J. Honigmann, 'The Masked Face,' *Ethos* (1977).
Al Ridenour, *The Krampus and the Old, Dark Christmas* (2016).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Perchten masks, Salzburg
entries.creator: Anonymous (Alpine mask-carvers)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Perchten masks from Salzburg, the masked figures of the Alpine winter processions associated with the spirit Frau Perchta.

## Iconography
Grotesque carved **masks** — some beautiful (*Schönperchten*), some hideous and horned (*Schiachperchten*) — worn in processions through the dark midwinter weeks.

## Significance
The **Perchten** take their name from **Frau Perchta**, the Alpine winter spirit who rewards the diligent and disembowels the slovenly during the *Rauhnächte* ('rough nights') between Christmas and Epiphany. Their masked runs are [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] rites meant to drive out winter, banish evil spirits, and ensure fertility for the coming year. Beautiful and ugly Perchten embody the year's two faces. The custom is a key case-study in the survival of pre-Christian winter ritual, discussed by Carlo Ginzburg.

## For artists & game designers
Paired 'fair' and 'foul' masks for a single winter-spirit — a built-in design dichotomy for a seasonal entity.

citations.text: Carlo Ginzburg, *Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath* (1991).
John J. Honigmann, 'The Masked Face,' *Ethos* (1977).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Schiachperchten table-mask, Goldegg Perchtenlauf
entries.creator: Anonymous (Alpine mask-carver); photograph by Wald1siedel
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Wald1siedel). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A towering Perchten table-mask (*Tafelmaske*) from the Goldegg Perchtenlauf in Salzburg, an elaborate form of the Alpine procession mask.

## Iconography
A huge, vertically built **frame-mask** rising far above the wearer's head, crowned with horns, fur, mirrors, and carved faces — a *Tafelmaske* (table-mask) of monumental scale carried in the run.

## Significance
This form belongs to the *Perchtenlauf* of the Pongau region, where the massive masks are displayed and paraded during the *Rauhnächte*. Like the simpler [[motif:perchten|Perchten]] and [[motif:krampus|Krampus]] masks it is [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] — its noise, height, and terrifying faces meant to drive out winter and evil and to bring luck and fertility. The Goldegg run is among the best-known surviving examples of the tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A mask that becomes architecture — a person-sized 'walking shrine' of horns and faces, perfect for a festival-boss design.

citations.text: Carlo Ginzburg, *Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath* (1991).
Al Ridenour, *The Krampus and the Old, Dark Christmas* (2016).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: SATOR square, carved stone at Oppède
entries.creator: Anonymous (medieval mason); photograph by M Disdero
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: M Disdero). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A SATOR square carved in stone at Oppède, the famous five-word Latin palindrome used as a protective charm.

## Iconography
A 5×5 grid of letters spelling **SATOR / AREPO / TENET / OPERA / ROTAS** — a square that reads the same across, down, and (reversed) both ways. Here it is incised into a block of building stone.

## Significance
The **SATOR square** (or ROTAS square) is one of the most enduring [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] word-charms of the West. Its letters can be rearranged into a cross of *PATERNOSTER* flanked by A and O (Alpha and Omega), giving it a Christian reading, while its palindromic perfection made it a charm against fire, illness, and the Devil from antiquity onward. It was cut into walls, written on bread and slips, and copied in charm-books across Europe.

## For artists & game designers
A self-mirroring letter-grid that works as a charm by its very symmetry — a ready-made 'word-puzzle ward'.

citations.text: Duncan Fishwick, 'On the Origin of the Rotas-Sator Square,' *Harvard Theological Review* (1964).
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: SATOR (ROTAS) square graffito, Pompeii
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman); recorded by Matteo Della Corte
entries.rights: Public domain (record: Matteo Della Corte). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A record of the SATOR/ROTAS word-square scratched at Pompeii, among the earliest known examples of the charm, buried by Vesuvius in 79 CE.

## Iconography
The drawing reproduces the **5×5 Latin word-square** (here in the ROTAS-first orientation) recorded from the Palaestra and other Pompeian walls (CIL IV 8623, 8622).

## Significance
Because Pompeii was destroyed in **79 CE**, its SATOR graffiti prove the [[motif:sator square|word-square]] existed in the first century, long before its medieval Christian use — fuelling debate over whether its origins were Jewish, Christian, Mithraic, or simply a clever Latin palindrome later given sacred meaning. As the earliest dated witnesses, the Pompeian examples anchor the entire history of this travelling charm. Recorded by the archaeologist Matteo Della Corte.

## For artists & game designers
An ancient wall-scratch that is the 'first appearance' of a charm later found across Europe — a great deep-history Easter egg.

citations.text: Duncan Fishwick, 'On the Origin of the Rotas-Sator Square,' *Harvard Theological Review* (1964).
Rebecca Benefiel, 'Magic Squares and the Pompeian Graffiti,' in studies of ancient graffiti.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Abracadabra triangle amulet diagram
entries.creator: Anonymous (after Serenus Sammonicus)
entries.date: charm of late antiquity; later diagram
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The classic abracadabra charm written as a diminishing triangle, a word-amulet against fever and disease.

## Iconography
The word **ABRACADABRA** written on successive lines, each dropping a final letter, so the text **tapers** to a single A and forms an inverted triangle. The whole was inscribed on parchment and worn at the neck.

## Significance
The **abracadabra** charm is first recorded in the *Liber Medicinalis* of Quintus Serenus Sammonicus (3rd c. CE), who prescribed the diminishing triangle to drive out fever: as the word shrank, so the illness was to dwindle and depart. It is a textbook case of [[motif:word magic|word-magic]] — the form of the writing, not just its meaning, doing the work — and circulated as a [[motif:apotropaic|protective]] amulet through the Middle Ages and into early-modern charm-books.

## For artists & game designers
A spell that 'counts down' a disease by shedding a letter each line — an elegant visual mechanic for a tapering healing charm.

citations.text: Quintus Serenus Sammonicus, *Liber Medicinalis* (3rd c. CE).
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Ethiopian magic scroll (prayers against ailments), Wellcome
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ethiopian dabtara / scribe)
entries.rights: Public domain. Wellcome Collection, via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An Ethiopian protective scroll comprising prayers against various ailments, from the Wellcome Collection — a body-length amulet of the Ethiopian magico-religious tradition.

## Iconography
A long, narrow strip of **parchment** written in **Ge'ez** with prayers, divine and angelic names, and bordered by **talismanic paintings** — staring guardian faces, geometric 'net' designs, and angels — meant to bind demons and the spirits of disease.

## Significance
Such **magic scrolls** (*ya branna ṭelsam*) were made by the *dabtara* (clergy-scribes) to the height of their owner, to heal and protect against disease, the [[motif:evil eye|evil eye]], and possession by spirits such as the *buda*. The interlaced eyes and faces are characteristic [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] devices. They form one of the richest living traditions of [[motif:goetia_grimoire|written]] protective magic, studied by Jacques Mercier.

## For artists & game designers
A scroll measured to the owner's own body, painted with watching eyes — a deeply personal 'protective relic' with a vivid graphic language.

citations.text: Jacques Mercier, *Ethiopian Magic Scrolls* (1979).
Wellcome Collection, L0031387.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Himmelsbrief (heaven-letter), c. 1800
entries.creator: Druck: Oemigke & Riemschneider, Neuruppin
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A coloured Himmelsbrief or 'letter from heaven', a printed protective broadside of German and Pennsylvania-German folk belief.

## Iconography
A printed sheet headed by a coloured **angel**, beneath which runs the text of a **letter purportedly sent from heaven** (often said to have fallen at Holstein or written by Christ's own hand), promising protection to whoever carries or copies it.

## Significance
The **Himmelsbrief** ('heaven-letter') is a Christian protective charm in epistolary form: kept in the house or carried on the body, it was believed to guard the bearer against fire, flood, weapons, and sudden death. Hugely popular in German lands and carried by emigrants to Pennsylvania, where it joined the *Brauche* (pow-wow) repertoire alongside the [[motif:long lost friend|Long Lost Friend]]. It belongs to the wider family of amuletic protective texts.

## For artists & game designers
A holy letter that protects simply by being carried — a charm that is pure text plus an angel, ideal as a 'blessed document' item.

citations.text: Don Yoder, 'The Himmelsbrief,' in *Discovering American Folklife* (1990).
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *Der lange verborgene Freund* (The Long Lost Friend), Hohman
entries.creator: Johann Georg Hohman
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The title page of Johann Georg Hohman's *Der lange verborgene Freund* (1820), the foundational charm-book of Pennsylvania-German pow-wow magic, later famed in English as *The Long Lost Friend*.

## Iconography
A plain letterpress **title page** in German, naming the book and its compiler, **Johann Georg Hohman**, printed at Reading, Pennsylvania. The book itself is a pocket-sized collection of recipes and charms.

## Significance
*The Long Lost Friend* is the most important printed source of **pow-wow** (*Brauche*), the Christian folk-healing and protective magic of the Pennsylvania Germans. It gathers charms against fire, theft, illness, and bewitchment — including the [[motif:sator square|SATOR square]] and biblical formulae — drawn from older German books like the *Romanus-Büchlein*. It became a near-talismanic object in its own right, said to protect the house that held it. Studied by Don Yoder and David Kriebel.

## For artists & game designers
A humble pocket book that is itself believed to protect its owner — a 'living grimoire' artifact rooted in real Americana.

citations.text: Johann Georg Hohman, *Der lange verborgene Freund* (Reading, PA, 1820).
David W. Kriebel, *Powwowing among the Pennsylvania Dutch* (2007).
